$GLD Goldman Sachs: “Gold Is Not Done”—Why Wall Street Still Sees Upside

Gold has pulled back sharply from its record highs, but Goldman Sachs believes the bull market is far from over. Despite a nearly 30% decline from January’s peak, the bank continues to forecast $4,900 per ounce by year-end, implying roughly 22% upside from current levels.

The message from Goldman is clear: the recent correction is viewed as a pause, not the end of the long-term bull market.

Why Has Gold Fallen?

Gold’s weakness has been driven primarily by higher inflation and rising interest-rate expectations.

As oil prices increased and inflation concerns returned, investors began pricing in the possibility that central banks may keep interest rates higher for longer.

Because gold pays no interest or dividend, higher bond yields make fixed-income investments relatively more attractive, putting short-term pressure on bullion prices.

This explains why gold, silver, platinum, copper, and palladium have all struggled over recent months.

Why Goldman Remains Bullish

Goldman argues that several powerful long-term drivers remain intact.

The biggest is central bank demand.

According to the World Gold Council, 45% of central banks surveyed plan to increase their gold reserves over the next 12 months, the highest level on record.

Central banks continue buying gold because it provides:

• A hedge against geopolitical uncertainty.

• Diversification away from the U.S. dollar.

• Protection during periods of financial instability.

Unlike retail investors, central banks typically buy for strategic reasons and often hold their positions for many years, creating a stable source of demand.

Structural vs. Cyclical Drivers

Goldman believes gold benefits from both structural and cyclical forces.

Structural drivers include continued central bank accumulation, rising government debt, and long-term demand for safe-haven assets.

Cyclical drivers could emerge if economic growth slows, inflation eases, or central banks eventually begin cutting interest rates. Lower interest rates reduce the opportunity cost of holding gold, historically supporting higher prices.

What Investors Should Watch

The next major catalysts for gold include:

• Federal Reserve interest-rate decisions.

• Inflation data.

• Central bank gold purchases.

• U.S. dollar strength.

• Geopolitical developments.

If interest rates remain elevated for longer than expected, gold could continue facing short-term pressure. However, if monetary policy becomes more accommodative, the precious metal could regain momentum.

Bottom Line

Goldman’s outlook suggests the recent selloff reflects changing interest-rate expectations rather than a collapse in the long-term investment thesis.

While short-term volatility is likely to continue, the bank believes structural demand—particularly from central banks—remains strong enough to support higher prices over time.

For investors, gold continues to serve a unique role within a diversified portfolio. It may not generate earnings or cash flow, but during periods of inflation, geopolitical uncertainty, and financial market stress, it has historically acted as an important store of value.

Whether gold reaches $4,900 will depend largely on the path of inflation and interest rates, but Goldman Sachs believes the precious metal’s long-term bull market is not over yet.

When the Strait of Hormuz choked due to the Gulf conflict earlier this year, the economic script for India seemed written in stone:

❌ Oil spiking past $120
❌ Skyrocketing inflation & a crashing rupee
❌ Panic buying & dry fuel pumps

After all, India imports 90% of its crude oil and over half its LPG. But instead of disaster, something unusual happened: Nothing. No lines at petrol pumps. No empty kitchens. No 1991 or 2013-style macroeconomic panic. Why? Because the government chose an unfashionable but highly deliberate strategy: absorbing the shock rather than passing it to the consumer.

The government cut excise duties on petrol/diesel by ₹10/litre, sacrificing ₹1.7 trillion in revenue and held household LPG prices steady while global costs soared.

Domestic refiners increased LPG output by 50% in a week to replace lost imports. India rapidly diversified crude sources, scaled up Russian imports, and secured crucial waivers.

Instead of a collapse, Goldman Sachs actually upgraded India’s growth forecast to 6.8% for CY26. Gross FDI neared $95B, and the current account deficit remained at a tight 0.6% of GDP.

Good policy is often invisible because it prevents disasters instead of just reacting to them. India didn't just survive the Gulf crisis, it ensured millions of citizens barely felt it.

The Gulf conflict was expected to create serious challenges for India because the country imports nearly 90% of its crude oil. Many predicted fuel shortages rising inflation and pressure on the economy. Instead India showed the strength of years of planning.

• Fuel stations continued operating without disruption.

• LPG supplies remained available across the country.

• Refiners increased LPG production by nearly 50%.

• Crude imports were diversified to reduce dependence on one route.

• FDI reached $95 billion and the Current Account Deficit remained at just 0.6% of GDP.

• Goldman Sachs raised India's FY26 growth forecast to 6.8%.
India proved that resilience is not built during a crisis. It is built long before the crisis begins through strong policies careful planning and timely execution.

Australian Shares End Higher as Gold Rebounds, But Weekly Losses Persist 

Australian equities finished Friday modestly higher, supported by a rebound in precious metals and energy prices, although the market still recorded a weekly decline as global concerns over higher US interest rates continued to weigh on investor sentiment. The S&P/ASX 200 rose 0.2% to close at 8,764.2, while the broader All Ordinaries gained 0.1%. Despite the positive finish, the benchmark index fell 0.7% over the week and remains up only 0.6% for the year. 

The materials sector led the advance as gold prices recovered from an eight-month low. Gold climbed back above US$4,000 an ounce, prompting a strong rally across the sector, with all of the ASX 200’s top-performing stocks coming from gold mining. Kingsgate Consolidated, Ramelius Resources and Perseus Mining were among the strongest performers. Major diversified miners also benefited, with BHP, Rio Tinto and Fortescue posting solid gains as commodity prices stabilised. 

Energy stocks also strengthened after Brent crude rebounded to around US$73.80 a barrel, supporting gains in Woodside, Santos and Whitehaven Coal. 

Technology, however, remained under pressure following weakness in US mega-cap technology stocks amid renewed concerns over elevated valuations and the prospect of further Federal Reserve rate hikes. Data centre operator NEXTDC and Megaport were among the weakest performers. Healthcare also lagged as CSL fell sharply.

The People's Bank of China conducted its first overnight reverse repurchase operation, providing liquidity without setting a fixed rate.

The U.S. Supreme Court ruled that Trump cannot temporarily remove Federal Reserve Governor Cook.
Iran stated it has no plans to engage in negotiations with the United States in the coming days.

South Korea launched its "Three Mega Projects," investing quadrillions of won to bolster AI and semiconductor industries.

Samsung, SK Hynix, Micron, and other major memory chip manufacturers face antitrust class actions in the United States.

Global equities rose Monday, with the Nasdaq up 2%, and China's A-share pharmaceutical and semiconductor sectors surged.

Top Stories

The State Council's standing meeting heard a report on the development of artificial intelligence. The meeting emphasized the need to deeply understand the evolution trends of AI, improve supportive policies and governance frameworks, and firmly grasp the initiative in development. It called for intensified efforts to achieve breakthroughs in AI innovation, accelerate key technology research and the construction of ultra-large-scale intelligent computing clusters, and fully implement the "AI+" initiative. It also stressed the importance of safeguarding AI security by establishing a dynamic, tiered, and classified regulatory system. The meeting reviewed the current foreign trade situation and work on building a trade powerhouse, and approved the "14th Five-Year Plan for Carbon Peaking" and the "14th Five-Year Plan for National Health."

The South Korean government, in collaboration with semiconductor giants Samsung Electronics and SK Group, unveiled three "super projects" centered on semiconductors, physical AI, and AI data centers, with total investments exceeding 47.55 trillion won—the largest-scale investment in Korean history. South Korea aims to build a domestic AI ecosystem to support its development over the next two to three decades. Korean officials project that DRAM production capacity will double within five years, and the global memory market is expected to quadruple over the same period.

Samsung Electronics, SK Hynix, and Micron Technology are facing a class-action lawsuit in the United States, accused of colluding to manipulate prices of memory chips. Plaintiffs allege that the three firms, which produce the vast majority of global DRAM chips, coordinated supply and pricing since 2022, driving DRAM prices up by approximately 700% over the past four years. While the lawsuit remains relatively limited in scope, it could expand significantly if the court accepts the plaintiffs' claims and certifies it as a class action.

Iranian Foreign Ministry spokesman Baghaei stated that Iran has no plans to engage in negotiations with the United States in the coming days. An Iranian expert delegation will travel to Doha to follow up on the implementation of the Islamabad Memorandum of Understanding, but this visit is unrelated to the U.S. delegation's scheduled trip to Qatar. U.S. President Trump insists talks will take place on June 30 in Doha. White House Press Secretary Levitt said U.S. special envoy Witkoff and Trump's son-in-law Kushner "will fly to Doha this week" for the talks.

China Macro & Policy

The State Council issued the "14th Five-Year Plan for Education Development," outlining systematic deployments to improve the forward-looking layout of educational resources, strengthen regional educational distribution, and optimize talent cultivation structures. The plan identifies six comprehensive reform tasks, including deepening educational evaluation reform. It explicitly explores extending the duration of compulsory education and advancing AI education across all educational stages to enhance students' AI literacy and problem-solving capabilities. It also calls for upgrading and expanding higher education, with an increase of more than 1 million undergraduate admissions at "Double First-Class" universities.

China's Ministry of Commerce announced the inclusion of 20 Japanese entities on an export control list, prohibiting Chinese exporters from shipping dual-use items to these entities and barring foreign organizations and individuals from transferring or providing Chinese-origin dual-use items to them. All ongoing activities involving these entities must cease immediately. The Ministry also placed the same 20 Japanese entities on a watch list.

The People's Bank of China conducted 157.5 billion yuan in seven-day reverse repurchase operations and 300 billion yuan in overnight reverse repurchase operations on June 29, resulting in a net cash drain of 19 billion yuan. This marked the first actual implementation of overnight reverse repurchase operations. Notably, the PBOC disclosed only the volume of the overnight operation, not the interest rate. Market participants believe this reflects the PBOC's intention to manage market expectations and prevent overinterpretation of policy signals during the initial phase of the tool's deployment.

China Equities Markets

A-shares rose steadily, with the SSE Index closing up 1.16% at 4,073.90, the Shenzhen Component Index up 0.19%, the ChiNext Index up 0.54%, the Star 50 Index surging 4.61%, and the Wind All China Index up 0.71%. Market turnover reached 3.54 trillion yuan. AI hardware showed divergent trends: fiberglass, copper-clad laminate, and optical communications sectors adjusted, while silicon wafers and memory segments continued their strong rally. Innovative pharmaceuticals surged, with gains also seen in agriculture, food and beverage, non-bank financial services, and coal sectors.

Hong Kong equities closed sharply higher: the Hang Seng Index rose 1.57% to 23,026.68, the Hang Seng Tech Index jumped 3.23%, and the Hang Seng China Enterprises Index gained 1.94%. Innovative pharmaceuticals led gains, followed by semiconductors and consumer sectors, while hardware equipment underperformed. Tech giants Tencent Holdings, Alibaba, and Meituan rebounded after prior underperformance. Net selling by Southbound funds exceeded 10.3 billion Hong Kong dollars, but Tencent was bought by over 1.5 billion Hong Kong dollars.

China Securities Index Company will launch the CSI Smart New Power System 100 Index on June 30, selecting 100 listed companies operating in six core sectors of the new power system: power generation, grid infrastructure, load management, energy storage, power system intelligence, carbon trading services, and equipment manufacturing.

The global top-performing memory ETF, Roundhill Memory ETF (DRAM), recently rebalanced its portfolio, adding China's memory chip giant GigaDevice for the first time, with a weighting of 2.91%, making it the ETF's eighth-largest holding.

Sources say Baidu's AI chip subsidiary Kunlun Tech plans to list in Hong Kong with a target valuation of approximately 50 billion U.S. dollars. Sources indicate Tencent has become a customer of Kunlun Tech, and ByteDance is also considering its chips. However, individuals close to ByteDance said the company currently has no intention to collaborate with Kunlun.

China Industry News

Nearly 20 analog and power semiconductor companies globally are set to initiate a new round of price hikes on July 1, continuing a pattern of multi-stage, incremental adjustments this year. Many manufacturers report robust order backlogs and significantly improved capacity visibility.

Guangzhou issued its first fine for "hidden area" construction in real estate. The Liwan District Urban Management Bureau penalized a housing development project for illegally converting decorative columns into structural columns without planning approval, altering nearly 200 square meters.

China's National Healthcare Security Administration published a preliminary list of drugs that passed initial review for the 2026 drug catalog, with 557 and 54 drugs passing initial review for the basic medical insurance and commercial insurance innovation drug lists, respectively. The overall preliminary review pass rate was 92%.

Three national standards—"Safety Requirements for Electric Vehicles," "Safety Requirements for Power Batteries for Electric Vehicles," and "Occupant Protection in Side Collisions"—will take effect on July 1. Industry experts say the new standards will fundamentally shift automotive competition from price-based to value-based.

The National Supercomputing Internet Initiative launched the "10,000 GPU Collaborators Incentive Program," offering up to billion-token-scale computational resources and thousands of GPU-hours of domestic computing power. The program invites collaborative projects in AI and AI for Science, including best practices, paper readings, training livestreams, developer competitions, and AI application incubation.

Fintech

The Bank for International Settlements (BIS), in its latest annual report, warned that AI bubble bursting, inflation resurgence, and fiscal pressure are the three core threats to global prosperity. The report specifically highlighted risks in AI financing structures, particularly so-called "circular financing" arrangements.

Fintech unicorn Airwallex announced it raised $320 million in its H-round funding, valuing the company at $11 billion—a 38% increase from six months ago. The company is doubling down on AI-driven financial software.

Corporate News & Updates

The DeepSeek team announced that DeepSeek V4 will be officially launched in mid-July, introducing a peak-off-peak pricing strategy. During peak hours, API pricing will be double the standard rate, which remains unchanged from the current V4 pricing.

Ningde Times' Jiexiawo mine has received its safety production license. Industry insiders said all production-related permits have now been secured, with the critical safety license obtained on the 29th.

Elon Musk announced that xAI's latest large model, Grok 4.5, has entered beta testing internally at SpaceX and Tesla. Early evaluations suggest its performance is comparable to, or possibly exceeds, Anthropic's Claude Opus. xAI plans to release a new large model every month for the remainder of the year.

Australian AI infrastructure firm Firmus signed a strategic partnership with NVIDIA to build a dedicated 360-megawatt NVIDIA DSX AI factory campus on Batam Island, Indonesia. Firmus expects customer commitments to generate $25 billion to $30 billion in revenue.

Affected by disruptions in Middle Eastern operations and fierce competition in China, Toyota's global sales fell 7.2% year-over-year to 834,300 units, marking its fourth consecutive monthly decline. Sales in China dropped 31.7% to 102,300 units. Honda's global sales fell 4.9%, while Nissan's declined 10.3%.

Global media, entertainment, and telecommunications giant Comcast announced plans to spin off its media and telecommunications businesses into two independent, publicly traded companies via a tax-free separation, enabling each to pursue distinct strategic priorities.

U.S. commercial space company Rocket Lab agreed to acquire satellite communications operator Iridium Communications in a deal valued at approximately $8 billion. The acquisition will help the company bypass three major barriers to building a satellite communications business.

Global Macro & Policy

The U.S. Supreme Court ruled 6-3 to overturn a nearly 90-year-old administrative law precedent, significantly expanding the president's power to dismiss officers of independent federal agencies. The ruling permits Trump to remove FTC Democratic commissioner Rebecca Kelly Slaughter, despite statutory restrictions requiring removal only for specific causes. Notably, the Court explicitly excluded the Federal Reserve from the ruling's scope, preserving its independence as previously established in May.

The U.S. Supreme Court denied President Trump's request to immediately remove Federal Reserve Governor Cook, but did not rule on whether Trump ultimately has the authority to dismiss Fed governors. Trump responded that he would ensure Cook does not make any significant monetary policy decisions.

Kevin Hassett, Director of the U.S. National Economic Council, said all available indicators suggest Thursday's nonfarm payrolls report will show "another strong number." He added that the surge in AI-driven productivity has a deflationary effect, reducing the justification for further rate hikes.

A joint working group from Oman and Iran held its first meeting on the Strait of Hormuz, exchanging views on its future management and related issues. Oman's Foreign Minister Badr stated Oman does not support charging transit fees for vessels but did not rule out exploring maritime service mechanisms. Iran's Deputy Foreign Minister Gharibbadi emphasized that all vessels must follow Iran's designated route through the Strait, warning that Iran will respond to any deviation.

Israeli Defense Minister Katz, in a closed-door meeting with military journalists, stated that the IDF is prepared to launch an independent military strike against Iran and reaffirmed Israel's intent to maintain a long-term military presence in southern Lebanon. He emphasized, however, that Israel will not interfere with President Trump's actions regarding Iran.

Global Equity Markets

U.S. major indexes closed higher: the Dow Jones Industrial Average rose 0.59% to 52,182.74, the S&P 500 gained 1.18% to 7,440.43, and the Nasdaq Composite surged 2.07% to 25,820.14. Caterpillar and Cisco led Dow gains, both up over 3%. Technology stocks drove the rally: the Wind US Magnificent Seven Index rose 2.05%, Tesla jumped over 8%, and Google rose nearly 5%. SpaceX gained over 7%. The Nasdaq China Golden Dragon Index rose 1.41%, Baidu surged over 7%, and Hesai Tech climbed over 6%.

European major indexes closed slightly lower: Germany's DAX fell 0.18% to 24,626.89, France's CAC 40 dropped 0.21% to 8,367.33, and the UK's FTSE 100 declined 0.23% to 10,484.22. Profit-taking due to elevated tech valuations and uncertainty around Fed policy weighed on market sentiment.

Asian markets showed mixed performance: Japan's Nikkei 225 closed up 0.15% at 69,468.11. South Korea's KOSPI fell 0.2% to 8,394.65, but the KOSDAQ surged 8.13%, briefly triggering a circuit breaker. Foreign investors sold a record 7.7 trillion won in Korean equities on Monday. India's S&P BSE Sensex declined 0.48% to 76,728.37.

Bonds

China's interbank bond market remained strong, with yields on major government bonds declining across the curve, with one-year yields falling about 3 basis points. Treasury futures rallied, with the 30-year contract up 0.12%. Market liquidity remained comfortably loose, with the PBOC net draining 19 billion yuan. The debut of overnight reverse repos sparked speculation that the rate may be set far below prior market expectations.

U.S. Treasury yields moved mixed: the two-year yield rose 2.26 basis points to 4.106%, the three-year yield rose 2.53 basis points to 4.107%, the five-year yield rose 1.92 basis points to 4.143%, the 10-year yield rose 1.17 basis points to 4.374%, and the 30-year yield dipped 0.10 basis point to 4.863%.

Commodities

International precious metals futures generally fell: COMEX gold futures dropped 1.61% to $4,030.50 per ounce, and COMEX silver futures fell 1.54% to $58.76 per ounce. The Supreme Court's ruling bolstering Fed independence, combined with hawkish Fed signals reinforcing tightening expectations, pressured precious metals prices, with only limited support from geopolitical uncertainty.

U.S. crude oil futures rose 1.72% to $70.42 per barrel; Brent crude rose 1.34% to $73.57 per barrel. Escalating tensions between the U.S. and Iran, coupled with slowed energy transit through the Strait of Hormuz, led markets to reassess assumptions of rapid supply recovery. Actual supply was constrained by vessel congestion, and futures had been oversold, pushing prices higher.

Most base metals on the LME closed lower: LME zinc rose 0.43% to $3,487.00 per tonne; LME copper fell 0.10% to $13,344.50; LME tin fell 0.40% to $50,350.00; LME lead fell 0.58% to $1,892.50; LME nickel fell 1.79% to $16,400.00; LME aluminum fell 2.55% to $3,098.50.

Russian President Putin, during a meeting on domestic fuel market stability, said systemic measures are needed to increase fuel supply and maintain economically reasonable prices. He also indicated the government is considering a full ban on diesel exports.

Wood Mackenzie forecasts that EU storage facilities may reach only 76% capacity by the end of the key gas refill season (April to October), the lowest peak level since at least 2011, according to data from the European Gas Infrastructure Organization.

FX & Currency Markets

Onshore renminbi closed at 6.7929 per U.S. dollar at 16:30, up 68 basis points from the previous close, and settled at 6.7960 in the night session. The PBOC's mid-rate was set at 6.8175, a 9-basis-point depreciation from the prior day.

At New York close, the U.S. dollar index fell 0.24% to 101.11. Most non-U.S. currencies rose: the euro gained 0.34% to 1.1424, the pound rose 0.48% to 1.3260, the Australian dollar fell 0.12% to 0.6887, the dollar-yen rose 0.12% to 161.9360, the dollar-loonie rose 0.12% to 1.4211, the dollar-franc fell 0.29% to 0.8075, and offshore renminbi gained 40 basis points to 6.8009.

The yen fell to 161.97 per U.S. dollar, breaching the 161.95 level at which Japan intervened in July 2024, marking the lowest level since December 1986. Japan's record $780 billion intervention a month ago has now been fully unwound.

From ChatGPT: No. The claim that the United States is down to "only 4 weeks of oil" is misleading unless someone is referring to a very specific stockpile and ignoring normal production and imports.

There are three different things people often confuse:

The Strategic Petroleum Reserve (SPR) — the government's emergency crude oil https://t.co/eKarbWnAvb of late June 2026, the SPR held roughly 330 million barrels, the lowest level since the early 1980s after recent emergency releases.
The U.S. consumes about 20 million barrels of petroleum per day, so 330 million barrels equals about 16–17 days of total U.S. consumption if it were the only source.

Commercial inventories — oil held by companies.U.S. commercial crude inventories were around 412 million barrels in late June 2026.
Adding commercial stocks to the SPR gives more than 700 million barrels of crude in storage, though not all of it is immediately usable in an emergency.

Actual U.S. energy supply.The United States is one of the world's largest oil producers, pumping roughly 13.8 million barrels per day, while also importing and exporting millions of barrels daily.
The country does not depend solely on stored reserves for its energy needs.

So, if someone says "America only has four weeks of oil left," that is generally false as a description of the nation's total energy position.
If they mean government emergency reserves plus certain assumptions about imports stopping and domestic production being disrupted, they may arrive at a figure of several weeks—but that is a specific worst-case scenario, not the normal reality. The U.S. continues to produce large quantities of oil every day and remains a major participant in global energy markets.
